seo.title: Government Agentic RAG AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for agentic rag AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying Agentic RAG systems in government contexts carries extraordinary stakes: autonomous query planning and multi-source synthesis can inadvertently expose classified data, produce unexplainable decisions affecting citizen rights, or generate hallucinated citations that corrupt official records. Federal agencies must satisfy overlapping mandates from FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and the Executive Order on AI before any agentic system touches production workloads. This checklist provides concrete, auditable controls mapped directly to government compliance requirements and the specific failure modes of multi-step retrieval agents.

sections.heading: Data Classification & Access Control
sections.description: Agentic RAG systems autonomously plan and execute queries across multiple data sources, making it critical to enforce strict data classification boundaries before any retrieval step occurs. A single misconfigured source connection can cause a classified document to be ingested by an external LLM, triggering national security violations.

items.label: Classify all retrieval sources before agent onboarding
items.description: Tag every document corpus, database, and API endpoint with a FISMA impact level (Low, Moderate, High) and verify that the agent's authorization boundary never spans classification levels without an approved cross-domain solution.

items.label: Block external LLM calls for Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) and above
items.description: Implement a hard policy gate in the agent orchestration layer that routes any query touching CUI-tagged sources exclusively to FedRAMP High-authorized, on-premises, or GovCloud-deployed models—never to commercial public endpoints.

items.label: Enforce attribute-based access control (ABAC) on every retrieval step
items.description: Integrate with the agency's existing ABAC or RBAC system so that each autonomous query the agent generates is evaluated against the requesting user's clearance level and need-to-know attributes before documents are retrieved.

items.label: Audit-log all source connections with user identity and timestamp
items.description: Capture a tamper-evident log entry for every retrieval action, including the query text, source identifier, document IDs returned, and the authenticated user or service account identity, stored in a SIEM that meets NIST 800-53 AU-2 requirements.

items.label: Implement data minimization in query planning
items.description: Configure the agent's query planner to retrieve only the minimum necessary document chunks required to answer the task, with a configurable maximum token budget per retrieval step to prevent bulk exfiltration patterns.

items.label: Validate source integrity with cryptographic checksums
items.description: Before the agent synthesizes a response, verify that each retrieved document chunk matches a stored hash to detect tampering or injection attacks on the retrieval corpus.

items.label: Conduct quarterly data source re-classification reviews
items.description: Schedule formal reviews where the information owner re-certifies the classification level of each source connected to the agent, documenting any changes in the system's Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M).

sections.heading: Algorithmic Accountability & Explainability
sections.description: Federal regulations and the Executive Order on AI require that AI-assisted decisions affecting citizens be explainable and appealable. Agentic RAG systems must expose their full reasoning chain—query planning steps, source selection rationale, and synthesis logic—in a format that non-technical reviewers and affected citizens can understand and challenge.

items.label: Complete an Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) before production deployment
items.description: Document the agent's decision logic, training data provenance, potential disparate impacts on protected classes, and mitigation measures using the agency's AIA template, obtaining sign-off from the Chief AI Officer and legal counsel.

items.label: Generate a human-readable reasoning trace for every agent response
items.description: Require the agent to output a structured explanation that lists each query planning step, which sources were consulted, why each source was selected, and how retrieved content was weighted in the final synthesis—stored alongside the response in the audit log.

items.label: Implement a citizen appeal workflow for agent-assisted decisions
items.description: Build a formal process where any citizen affected by a decision informed by the agent can request human review, with a documented SLA for response and a mechanism to override or annotate the agent's output in the official record.

items.label: Attach verified citations to every factual claim in agent output
items.description: Enforce a citation verification step that maps each sentence in the synthesized response to a specific document ID, page, and paragraph, and flags any claim that cannot be grounded in a retrieved source as unverified before the response is delivered.

items.label: Log query planning decisions with rationale for auditor review
items.description: Capture the agent's sub-goal decomposition and source selection decisions in a structured format that can be replayed by an auditor or inspector general to reconstruct exactly how the agent arrived at its output.

items.label: Publish a plain-language model card for the deployed agent
items.description: Create a public-facing or internally accessible model card describing the agent's capabilities, known limitations, data sources, and the types of decisions it is and is not authorized to inform, updated with each major version change.

items.label: Conduct annual bias and fairness audits on agent outputs
items.description: Analyze a statistically representative sample of agent responses across demographic groups to detect disparate accuracy, citation quality, or recommendation patterns, documenting findings and remediation actions.

sections.heading: Hallucination Detection & Source Faithfulness
sections.description: In government contexts, a hallucinated statute citation or fabricated policy reference can corrupt official decisions, trigger legal liability, and undermine public trust. Agentic RAG systems must implement multi-layer verification to ensure every claim is grounded in retrieved sources and that cross-source synthesis does not introduce contradictions.

items.label: Deploy an automated hallucination detection layer on all agent outputs
items.description: Integrate a secondary verification model or rule-based system that scores each factual claim in the agent's response against the retrieved source chunks, flagging claims with a faithfulness score below a defined threshold (e.g., 0.85) for human review before delivery.

items.label: Implement cross-source consistency checks before synthesis
items.description: After multi-source retrieval, run an automated consistency check that identifies contradictions between sources and requires the agent to explicitly surface conflicts in its response rather than silently resolving them, ensuring reviewers see the full information landscape.

items.label: Enforce a 'no citation, no claim' policy in agent output templates
items.description: Configure the agent's output schema to reject any factual assertion that does not include a verified citation pointer, returning an error state that triggers human escalation rather than delivering an uncited response.

items.label: Validate citation accuracy with exact-match retrieval verification
items.description: After the agent generates citations, run an automated step that re-retrieves the cited document and verifies that the quoted or paraphrased content actually appears in the source, logging mismatches as citation integrity failures.

items.label: Set confidence thresholds that trigger mandatory human review
items.description: Define confidence score thresholds for query planning accuracy and source faithfulness below which the agent must pause and route the task to a human analyst, with the threshold values documented in the system's security plan.

items.label: Run red-team adversarial retrieval tests quarterly
items.description: Conduct structured red-team exercises where testers attempt to inject misleading documents into retrieval corpora or craft queries designed to elicit hallucinated responses, documenting findings and patching the agent's guardrails accordingly.

items.label: Maintain a hallucination incident register with root-cause analysis
items.description: Log every detected hallucination or citation failure as a formal incident, perform root-cause analysis within 5 business days, and track remediation status in the agency's POA&M to demonstrate continuous improvement to auditors.

items.label: Test query planning accuracy against a labeled government benchmark dataset
items.description: Develop or adopt a benchmark of government-domain queries with known correct retrieval paths and evaluate the agent's query planner against this dataset before each major release, with accuracy metrics reported to the AI governance board.

sections.heading: FedRAMP Authorization & Operational Security
sections.description: Every component in the Agentic RAG stack—vector databases, orchestration frameworks, LLM inference endpoints, and logging infrastructure—must operate within an authorized FedRAMP boundary appropriate to the data sensitivity level. Operational security controls must address the unique attack surface created by autonomous agent behavior, including prompt injection and retrieval poisoning.

items.label: Verify FedRAMP High authorization for all infrastructure components
items.description: Confirm that every service in the agent stack (vector store, LLM endpoint, orchestration platform, logging system) holds a current FedRAMP High Authorization to Operate (ATO) or is covered under the agency's own ATO boundary, with no exceptions for 'temporary' or 'pilot' deployments handling sensitive data.

items.label: Implement prompt injection detection and sanitization
items.description: Deploy input validation and anomaly detection on all user-supplied queries and retrieved document content to identify and block prompt injection attempts that could redirect the agent's query planning or cause it to exfiltrate data through crafted outputs.

items.label: Enforce Section 508 accessibility compliance on all agent interfaces
items.description: Audit all user-facing interfaces for the agent—including response displays, citation panels, and appeal workflows—against WCAG 2.1 AA standards and the Revised 508 Standards, with documented test results from an independent accessibility evaluator.

items.label: Conduct a NIST 800-53 control mapping for the full agent architecture
items.description: Map every component and data flow in the Agentic RAG system to applicable NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls, document control implementation status in the System Security Plan (SSP), and obtain Authorizing Official (AO) approval before go-live.

items.label: Implement network segmentation between retrieval and synthesis components
items.description: Isolate the retrieval layer (vector database, document stores) from the LLM inference layer using network segmentation and zero-trust micro-perimeters, ensuring that a compromise of the synthesis component cannot directly access raw document stores.

items.label: Establish a continuous monitoring program with monthly POA&M updates
items.description: Stand up automated vulnerability scanning, configuration compliance checks, and anomaly detection for all agent components, with findings reviewed monthly and tracked in the POA&M submitted to the agency's ISSO and AO.

items.label: Define and test an incident response playbook specific to agent failures
items.description: Create a documented incident response playbook covering scenarios unique to agentic systems—runaway query loops, retrieval poisoning, unauthorized data synthesis—with defined escalation paths, containment procedures, and congressional notification thresholds.

items.label: Perform supply chain risk assessment on all third-party agent components
items.description: Apply NIST 800-161 supply chain risk management practices to all open-source libraries, model weights, and third-party APIs used in the agent stack, maintaining a software bill of materials (SBOM) and monitoring for vulnerabilities in each component.

notes.regulation: Executive Order on AI (EO 14110) & OMB M-24-10
notes.note: Federal agencies must complete an annual inventory of AI use cases and designate a Chief AI Officer accountable for each deployment. Agentic RAG systems that inform decisions affecting rights or safety are classified as 'rights-impacting' or 'safety-impacting' AI, triggering mandatory minimum risk practices including impact assessments, human oversight mechanisms, and public transparency reporting. Agencies must document compliance with these minimum practices or obtain a formal waiver before deployment.

FISMA requires every federal information system to obtain an Authority to Operate based on a risk assessment aligned to NIST 800-53 controls. For Agentic RAG, particular attention must be paid to control families SI (System and Information Integrity) for hallucination and injection controls, AU (Audit and Accountability) for agent action logging, and AC (Access Control) for retrieval boundary enforcement. The autonomous nature of the agent requires explicit documentation of how human oversight satisfies the 'least privilege' and 'separation of duties' controls.

Any Agentic RAG component processing data at the Moderate or High FISMA impact level must use FedRAMP-authorized services. Agencies must verify that the specific service offering—not just the vendor—holds a current ATO listed on the FedRAMP Marketplace, and that the authorized boundary explicitly covers the agentic use pattern (e.g., API-based LLM inference, vector database queries). Using a FedRAMP-authorized vendor's non-authorized product line is a common compliance gap that has triggered audit findings.

All electronic and information technology procured or developed by federal agencies must conform to the Revised 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194). For Agentic RAG deployments, this requires that agent-generated responses, citation displays, reasoning traces, and appeal interfaces be fully accessible to users with disabilities, including compatibility with screen readers, keyboard-only navigation, and sufficient color contrast. Agencies must obtain a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) from vendors and conduct independent accessibility testing before deployment.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your Agentic RAG system against FedRAMP, FISMA, and Executive Order requirements—automatically flagging hallucinations, citation failures, and access control violations before they become congressional inquiries.
